Diagnosing a run of fmri.pipeline

Zach Vig

15 August 2025

Source: vignettes/diagnosis.Rmd
diagnosis.Rmd

Overview of diagnose_pipeline Function

The diagnose_pipeline() function provides the user quick access to job tracking data, output files, and log files to help diagnose faulty pipeline runs. To use the function, supply it with either a ‘gpa’ object or a path string to a top-level project directory (containing a ‘project_config.json’ file, a ‘scheduler_scripts’ folder, an SQLite database, and other files used for an analysis). Note that when a ‘gpa’ object is supplied, the function will try to access the output locations specified in the object; if a top-level directory path is supplied, the function will search in conventionally named folders for applicable information.

In the example below, it is assumed a ‘gpa’ object called gpa has already been constructed in the R environment. To start a diagnosis, simply execute:

diagnose_pipeline(gpa)

You should receive a message that says

Running pipeline diagnosis...
(Hit ESC at any time to cancel)

indicating the diagnosis has launched successfully. Note that the ESC key can be used to exit the diagnosis at any time. In the unusual case that a specific element necessary to diagnose the pipeline (e.g., the SQLite database) is missing, the function will throw an informative error and cancel automatically.

Submission History

The diagnosis function will first print a history of the submissions of the current analysis including date/time, user, and steps submitted. For example, in the analysis below, the analysis has been submitted one time with all five possible steps included.

Example:

── Analysis: 23june2025 ───────────────────────────────────────────
• Output directory: /proj/23june2025
• Number of runs: 1
• History:
  1. Run on 07/31/25 at 08:58:49 AM by user (Latest)
    Finalize Config. → L1 Mods. → L2 Mods. → L3 Mods. → Clean Up

The function will then ask which run you would like to diagnose. Enter the integer of the run you would like to examine more closely, or hit enter to accept the default (the latest submission).

Example:

Which pipeline run would you like to diagnose?
Enter an integer (Press enter to accept default: 1) > 1

Status Print Out

Next, the diagnosis function will print the status of each job (and a summary of child job statuses) in the sequence:

  • “[✔] COMPLETED” means the job has successfully completed.
  • “[↗] QUEUED” means the job was submitted but has not started yet.
  • “[⋯] STARTED” means the job has started but has not completed yet.
  • “[✗] FAILED” means the job has failed, either because of an error within the step or because a parent/upstream job failed. In the latter case, this information will be provided.

Note: There are two types of “child” jobs in the pipeline: steps that are downstream of another but will not start until the previous step is completed (e.g., run L1 models will submit after setup L1 models has completed), or smaller, “sub-” jobs that are part of the step itself (e.g., separate feat jobs). These are specified by a child “level” – 1 for the former type and 2 for the latter type – in the tracking database. All jobs within a series of steps that are dependent on another (no matter the child level) are called a “chain,” collectively. A collection of chains/jobs in a single analysis is called a “sequence” and is given a unique sequence ID in the tracking database.

At this point, you can either examine one of the steps more closely to determine what went wrong in the case of failure, or return the full tracking tree if you initially assigned the diagnosis function to an object (which was not done in the example; more on this second choice below).

Example:

The run you selected had 1 independent chain of steps:
1. Finalize Configuration [✔] COMPLETED
2. Set Up L1 Models [✔] COMPLETED
3. Run L1 Models [✔] COMPLETED
  Child Jobs:
  └─ 100 of 100 [✔] COMPLETED
4. Set Up/Run L2 Models [✔] COMPLETED
5. Set Up/Run L3 Models [✔] COMPLETED
6. Clean Up Pipeline [✗] FAILED

Examining a Step

If you’d like to examine a step more closely, type ‘yes’ when prompted. You will receive a message asking which chain (if applicable) and then which step you’d like to examine. Enter the integer of the corresponding chain/step you’d like to examine. The example below had a single chain so the user only has to make the choice of which step.

Example:

Would you like to examine any of these steps more closely?
ℹ Type 'yes' to continue, or 'no' to return full job tree and exit now.
(yes/no) > yes
Which step would you like to examine more closely?
ℹ There are 6 steps in chain 1
Enter an integer > 6

The function will print out detailed information about the selected step (queue/start/end times, if applicable, downstream jobs, child jobs).

Example:

── Job `cleanup_fsl`...
• ...was submitted at 2025-07-31 14:50:14
• ...and was started at 2025-07-31 16:18:58
• ...failed before finishing
• ...had no downstream (dependent) jobs
• ...had no child jobs

Performing Actions

The diagnosis function will then list a series of actions that can possibly be performed to further diagnose any problems with the step. These actions could include some or all of the following:

  • View output file in console: This will print the R output file from the step in the console. The user can specify how many lines from the end to view (default: 15).
  • Return output file as character object: This will return the R output file from the step as a character object if the function was assigned to an object initially.
  • View child job details (statuses, etc.): If the step had level 2 child jobs (smaller, sub-jobs that are part of the step), this will print out a more detailed status table for each of those child jobs.
  • View feat status table: This will display which feat jobs have been completed and which have failed, with their corresponding ID.
  • View feat log: This will print a user-specified number (default: 15) of lines from the end of the corresponding log file for that step. It will also ask if the user wishes to view any individual-level logs by specifying the ID.
  • Exit diagnosis and return full tracking tree: This will end the diagnosis function and return the full tracking tree (see below)

Function Output/Tracking Tree

It can be beneficial to assign the initial function call to an object as in the example below.

output <- diagnose_pipeline(gpa)

In this case, the diagnosis function will be able to return helpful information corresponding to what the user specifies in the command line interface. One such piece information is the full job tracking tree, a readable version of the job tracking information stored in the SQLite database. The tree comes in a data structure from the data.tree package, an R package for working with nested data.

Printing the tree yields a readable structure with the top level being a sequence, then chains, then steps/jobs, and finally sub-jobs/level 2 child jobs.

Example:

                                  levelName
1  seq_ae2c17c7-95d8-4dc8-a8c8-e4532334ef47
2   °--chain1
3       ¦--finalize_configuration
4       ¦--setup_l1
5       ¦--run_l1
6       ¦   ¦--featsep_l1_1
7       ¦   ¦--featsep_l1_2
8       ¦   ¦--featsep_l1_3
9       ¦   ¦--featsep_l1_4
10      ¦   °--featsep_l1_5
11      ¦--setup_run_l2
12      ¦--setup_run_l3
13      °--cleanup_fsl

Accessible fields in the tree for each job include: batch_directory, batch_file, child_level, code_file, compute_file, head, id, job_id, job_name, job_obj, mem_total, n_cpus, n_nodes, scheduler, sequence_id, status, time_ended, time_started, time_submitted, wall_time, parent_id, scheduler_options, and mem_per_cpu. For example, simply execute print(output, "status") to view the status of each job in the tree.

Read the data.tree documentation for more information about working with this easy-to-use data structure.

On this page
